summary:
The syndrome with two XX sex chromosomes along with one Y chromosome is known as Klinefelter syndrome. This condition results from having an additional copy of the X chromosome compared to typical males who have XY chromosomal composition.
The other mentioned conditions such as Down or Turner do not fit this specific combination according to their defining characteristics based on available information about these genetic disorders. Thus, it can confidently be concluded that Klinefelter fits best given its unique chromosomal makeup involving both pairs of female-specific chromosomes alongside male specificity through presence of at least one Y-chromosome. To reiterate succinctly,

Klinefelter syndrome matches exactly what was asked due to possessing features including double set of females' sexual markers combined uniquely also carrying single instance of typically found marker among men indicating correct answer aligns precisely here without ambiguity regarding matching criteria provided initially within query context itself.
